paragraphs.text: Few places captured everyday Los Angeles quite like the 99 Cents Only Store. Fluorescent aisles packed with snacks, toys, household supplies, and strange one-off items created a shopping experience that felt chaotic, colorful, and uniquely local. For decades the stores became part of the city’s visual language. Now, as many locations disappear, artists are preserving that world in a different form

The exhibition treats the store itself as both subject and material. Bright price tags, product packaging, shelving systems, and signage become the foundation for installations and sculptures. Artists reinterpret the familiar visuals of the store and elevate them into playful commentary on consumer culture, nostalgia, and the strange beauty hidden in everyday retail environments.

Walking through the exhibition feels a lot like stepping back into the aisles themselves. Bold colors, repetitive packaging, and stacks of products create an overwhelming but oddly comforting environment. The difference is that here the objects are no longer for sale. They are reimagined as art, capturing a moment in time when the 99 Cents Only Store served as a small but unforgettable part of the
